slides.title: Review and Practice: Protecting Spaces
slides.subtitle: Protecting Spaces: one consolidating pass across both objectives — managerial controls (2.3.A) and physical controls (2.3.B) — then the Lesson 2.3 quiz
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome to Lesson 2.3, Day 4 of 4 — review and practice. There is no new content today. Across three days you've covered both objectives: 2.3.A, the managerial controls, security awareness training and the workstation security policy; and 2.3.B, the physical controls and how to choose them by prevent-detect-correct, applied to Scenario 2A and prioritized by severity and cost. Today we consolidate. We'll run a single worked table that puts both objectives side by side, do one cumulative pass through Xtensr that uses managerial and physical controls together, practice a Stop and Think under exam conditions, recap the vocabulary, and then take the Lesson 2.3 quiz. The goal today is speed and certainty, not new facts.

slides.prompt: Sort this mixed list of eight measures into MANAGERIAL (2.3.A) or PHYSICAL (2.3.B): security awareness training; bollards; a clean desk policy; locks on server cabinets; a workstation security policy; card readers; a privacy screen filter; disabling USB ports.
slides.subprompt: Write your two columns. Then circle the ONE item that surprised you or that you had to think hardest about, and write one sentence on why.
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own. Here are eight measures, and I want you to sort them into two columns: managerial, which is 2.3.A, and physical, which is 2.3.B. Security awareness training, bollards, clean desk policy, locks on server cabinets, workstation security policy, card readers, privacy screen filter, disabling USB ports. Write the two columns, then circle the one that made you stop and think and tell me why in a sentence. This is the single skill the exam tests most in this lesson — naming the category — so it's exactly where we start review.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 4 Review packet has three sections spanning both objectives. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: The Whole-Lesson Worked Table
sections.description: One table mapping every EK 2.3 control to its category and what it prevents, detects, or corrects (EK 2.3.A.1–A.2, 2.3.B.1–B.7).

sections.title: Cumulative Scenario 2A Pass
sections.description: Walk Xtensr once more using BOTH managerial and physical controls together on each gap (EK 2.3.B.1, 2.3.B.8).

sections.title: Exam Traps to Avoid
sections.description: The category mix-up, the unmatched control, and the unsorted wish list — the three errors the quiz is built to catch.

slides.note: Fill in your Review packet as we go. The Lesson 2.3 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Three review sections. Section one is the whole-lesson worked table — every control from both objectives, its category, and what it prevents, detects, or corrects, all in one place. Section two is a cumulative pass through Scenario 2A that deliberately uses managerial and physical controls together on each gap. Section three is an enrichment naming the three traps the quiz is built to catch. Keep your packet open; we move quickly today because none of it is new.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Sort any EK 2.3 control into its category — managerial (2.3.A) or physical (2.3.B) — even when the wording is a trap (LO 2.3.A)
Choose a control for a vulnerability, justify it with prevent/detect/correct, and prioritize the set by severity and cost (LO 2.3.B)
Avoid the three exam traps — the category mix-up, the unmatched control, and the unsorted wish list (LO 2.3.A, LO 2.3.B)

slides.script: Today consolidates both of the lesson's objectives. First, under 2.3.A: sort any control from the lesson into its category — managerial or physical — even when the wording is a trap, like a privacy screen that is hardware but belongs to a managerial policy. Second, under 2.3.B: choose a control for a vulnerability, justify it with prevent-detect-correct, and prioritize the whole set by severity and cost. Third, an exam-strategy goal spanning both objectives — avoid the three traps the quiz is built to catch: the category mix-up, the unmatched control, and the unsorted wish list. By the end of today you'll switch fluently between naming a category and choosing a control, and then you'll prove it on the quiz.

slides.label: The Whole-Lesson Worked Table
slides.script: Section one. Both objectives in one table. We map every control from the lesson to its category and to prevent, detect, or correct. Ten minutes — fill every cell, this is your one-page study sheet.

slides.heading: Every EK 2.3 Control at a Glance
slides.subheading: Managerial (2.3.A) and physical (2.3.B) controls together, with category and prevent/detect/correct.
rows: Prevent — trained people refuse the attack
Workstation security policy (lock, clean desk, privacy screen, UPS; tiers)
Fencing, gates, bollards around the building
Prevent — deter/stop physical and vehicle access
Locks on doors, server cabinets, computers
Prevent — stop devices being accessed or stolen
Detect + Prevent — log badges, deny unauthorized
Prevent — block malware from external drives
Correct — keep critical devices powered through an outage
slides.footer: AP TIP: Two columns the exam tests directly — CATEGORY (managerial vs. physical) and the PREVENT/DETECT/CORRECT role (EK 2.3.B.1). Card readers are the classic ‘both’: they detect by logging and prevent by denying.
slides.script: Here's your one-page study sheet — every control in the lesson, its EK, its category, and what it does. Top two rows are managerial, 2.3.A: security awareness training and the workstation security policy, both fundamentally preventive because they close gaps before an attack lands. The rest are physical, 2.3.B. Fences, gates, bollards, B.2 — prevent, deter access including vehicles. Locks, B.3 — prevent theft. Card readers, B.4 — and watch this one, it's the classic ‘both,’ it detects by logging which badge opened a door and prevents by denying unauthorized badges. Vestibules and turnstiles, B.5 — prevent admitting the wrong person. Disable USB ports, B.6 — prevent malware. UPS and generators, B.7 — correct, keep power flowing. Two columns the exam hits directly: category and the prevent-detect-correct role. Fill every cell; this is the sheet you study from.

slides.label: Cumulative Scenario 2A Pass
slides.script: Section two. One more walk through Xtensr — but this time, deliberately use managerial AND physical controls together on each gap. Twelve minutes.

slides.heading: Xtensr, One More Time: Managerial + Physical Together
slides.subheading: Cumulative pass. Each gap gets BOTH a managerial control (2.3.A) and a physical control (2.3.B) where possible.

left.title: Door and Entry Gaps
left.definition: People-and-policy plus hardware on the same gap
examples: Propped-open side door: train staff not to prop or badge others in (EK 2.3.A.1)
Add a vestibule or turnstile so the door physically can't admit a stranger (EK 2.3.B.5)
Add card readers to log and control restricted entries (EK 2.3.B.4)
Managerial fixes the habit; physical removes the option

right.title: Workstation and Device Gaps
right.definition: Policy plus hardware on the same gap
examples: Unlocked workstations and exposed screens: enforce the workstation policy — lock, clean desk, privacy screen (EK 2.3.A.2)
Lock server cabinets and computers so devices can't be taken (EK 2.3.B.3)
Disable USB ports on shared machines to block malware (EK 2.3.B.6)
Protect power with a UPS and generator for critical systems (EK 2.3.B.7)

slides.footer: KEY SKILL: The strongest Scenario 2A answer layers a MANAGERIAL control and a PHYSICAL control on the same gap — the habit and the hardware. Then prioritize the whole set by severity and cost (EK 2.3.B.8).
slides.script: Last pass through Xtensr, and the upgrade today is layering managerial with physical on the same gap. Left side, doors and entries: for the propped-open side door, train staff not to prop it or badge others in, EK 2.3.A.1 — that's the habit — and add a vestibule or turnstile so the door physically can't admit a stranger, EK 2.3.B.5 — that's the hardware — plus card readers, B.4, to log and control entry. Managerial fixes the habit; physical removes the option. Right side, workstations and devices: enforce the workstation policy — lock screens, clean desk, privacy filter, EK 2.3.A.2 — and lock the server cabinets and computers, B.3, disable USB ports, B.6, and protect power with a UPS and generator, B.7. Then, across the whole set, prioritize by severity and cost, EK 2.3.B.8. The strongest answer pairs the habit and the hardware on each gap and orders the recommendations.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Exam-Style Practice
slides.subheading: Both questions span LO 2.3.A and LO 2.3.B. Complete on your Review packet, then we debrief before the quiz.
prompts: A measure reads: ‘Require employees to lock their screens and clear sensitive papers before stepping away.’ Name the control, state its CATEGORY (managerial or physical), and explain why the category isn't ‘physical’ even though a locked screen involves hardware.

At Xtensr, the same propped-open door is the gap. Give ONE managerial control and ONE physical control that each mitigate its, and for each state whether it prevents, detects, or corrects. Then say which you'd fund first.
slides.directions: Independent. 7 minutes. Full sentences. We debrief answers before the quiz.
slides.script: Two questions, seven minutes, independent, full sentences — this is your dress rehearsal for the quiz. Question one tests the category trap: locking screens and clearing papers is the workstation security policy, EK 2.3.A.2, and its category is managerial — even though a screen lock involves hardware, the control is a policy, a rule people follow, which is why it's managerial not physical. Question two layers both objectives on the propped door: one managerial control, training, EK 2.3.A.1, and one physical, a vestibule, EK 2.3.B.5, each with prevent-detect-correct, then which you fund first by EK 2.3.B.8. Seven minutes, then we debrief, then the quiz.

slides.heading: Vocabulary Recap (LO 2.3.A and LO 2.3.B)

terms.definition: A control that protects a space through rules, policy, and training rather than hardware — security awareness training and the workstation security policy (EK 2.3.A.1).
A policy outlining measures to protect a workplace — lock devices, clean desk, privacy screen, surge/UPS — possibly in tiers by data sensitivity (EK 2.3.A.2).
A hardware or barrier-based control — fence, lock, card reader, vestibule, UPS — that physically deters, prevents, detects, or corrects an attack (EK 2.3.B.2).
The three roles a control can play; a defender chooses a relevant control by deciding which role fits the adversary's attack (EK 2.3.B.1).
An entry control that records which badges access which entries at what times and denies unauthorized badges — both detective and preventive (EK 2.3.B.4).
Ranking which mitigations to fund first based on the severity of the risks and the cost of the recommended mitigations (EK 2.3.B.8).

slides.script: Final vocabulary recap, the six terms most likely to appear on the quiz, pulled from both objectives. Managerial control — rules and training, EK 2.3.A.1. Workstation security policy and its requirements and tiers, EK 2.3.A.2. Physical control — the hardware family, EK 2.3.B.2. Prevent, detect, correct — the three roles, EK 2.3.B.1. Card reader — the detective-and-preventive entry control, EK 2.3.B.4. And risk prioritization — severity and cost, EK 2.3.B.8. If you know these six cold and can name a category and a prevent-detect-correct role for any control, you're ready for the quiz.

slides.script: Everything is on the site for final review. Run the Lesson 2.3 self-check one more time, then Exercise 7, a mixed drill on category and prevent-detect-correct, and Exercise 8, the full cumulative Scenario 2A recommendation. The quiz is the five-question check across both objectives, and you take it today, in class, right now. If you want any part of the lesson again afterward, the video walks it slide for slide.

slides.heading: End of Lesson 2.3
todayPoints: Consolidated both objectives in one worked table: every control by category and prevent/detect/correct (2.3.A + 2.3.B)
Ran a cumulative Scenario 2A pass layering managerial and physical controls on each gap
Practiced the category trap and the prevent/detect/correct justification under exam conditions
Recapped the key vocabulary and took the Lesson 2.3 quiz
nextPoints: Topic 2.4 begins: detecting physical attacks and placing monitoring equipment
Carry forward Scenario 2A — its fourth task is recommending monitoring placement
Keep the prevent/detect/correct model; detection controls are the ‘detect’ half
slides.teaser: You've learned to protect a space and to choose the right control for each gap. Next, Topic 2.4: how to know when a control has been beaten — detecting the attack — and where to place the monitoring that catches it, the last task in Scenario 2A.
slides.script: Lesson 2.3 is complete. You can identify the managerial controls in 2.3.A, determine mitigation strategies in 2.3.B, justify any control with prevent-detect-correct, apply all of it to Scenario 2A, and prioritize by severity and cost — and you've proven it on the quiz. Next is Topic 2.4, detecting physical attacks, which finishes Scenario 2A's fourth task: recommending where to place monitoring equipment to catch a breach. Notice it's the ‘detect’ half of the prevent-detect-correct model you've used all lesson. Keep your one-page worked table — it's the study sheet for the whole topic. Well done on Lesson 2.3.
